task: Write a Python function balanced(s) returning True when the parentheses in the string are balanced, considering only the characters '(' and ')' and ignoring everything else. A closing parenthesis must never appear before its opening partner: ')(' is not balanced. An empty string is balanced. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: The code correctly handles an empty string as balanced.
trace: I used a stack data structure to keep track of the opening parentheses. For each character in the input string, I added opening parentheses to the stack and checked if there were any closing parentheses. If there was a closing parenthesis, I first verified that there wasn't an empty stack or a mismatched closing parenthesis (i.e., a ')' before its matching '('). If everything was balanced, the stack would be empty at the end of the function. This approach is efficient and easy to understand as it mimics the behavior of how we think about balancing parentheses. The edge case handled here is when there are unmatched closing parentheses in the input string.
